extraction.title: Claude 3 Model Family

key_claims.statement: The Claude 3 model family consists of three models—Haiku, Sonnet, and Opus—in ascending order of capability.

key_claims.statement: Claude 3 Opus outperforms its peers on common AI evaluation benchmarks including MMLU, GPQA, and GSM8K.
key_claims.supporting_quote: Opus, our most intelligent model, outperforms its peers on most of the common evaluation benchmarks for AI systems, including undergraduate level expert knowledge (MMLU), graduate level expert reasoning (GPQA), basic mathematics (GSM8K), and more.

key_claims.statement: Claude 3 Sonnet is 2x faster than Claude 2 and Claude 2.1 while offering higher levels of intelligence.
key_claims.supporting_quote: For the vast majority of workloads, Sonnet is 2x faster than Claude 2 and Claude 2.1 with higher levels of intelligence.

key_claims.statement: Claude 3 models have sophisticated vision capabilities that can process photos, charts, graphs, and technical diagrams.
key_claims.supporting_quote: The Claude 3 models have sophisticated vision capabilities on par with other leading models. They can process a wide range of visual formats, including photos, charts, graphs and technical diagrams.

key_claims.statement: Claude 3 models are significantly less likely to refuse to answer prompts that border on the system's guardrails compared to previous generations.
key_claims.supporting_quote: Opus, Sonnet, and Haiku are significantly less likely to refuse to answer prompts that border on the system's guardrails than previous generations of models.

key_claims.statement: Opus demonstrates a twofold improvement in accuracy on complex, open-ended factual questions compared to Claude 2.1.
key_claims.supporting_quote: Compared to Claude 2.1, Opus demonstrates a twofold improvement in accuracy (or correct answers) on these challenging open-ended questions while also exhibiting reduced levels of incorrect answers.

key_claims.statement: Claude 3 models will initially offer a 200K context window upon launch, but are capable of accepting inputs exceeding 1 million tokens for select customers.
key_claims.supporting_quote: The Claude 3 family of models will initially offer a 200K context window upon launch. However, all three models are capable of accepting inputs exceeding 1 million tokens and we may make this available to select customers who need enhanced processing power.

key_claims.statement: Claude 3 Opus achieved over 99% accuracy on the Needle In A Haystack evaluation for recalling information from large corpuses.
key_claims.supporting_quote: Claude 3 Opus not only achieved near-perfect recall, surpassing 99% accuracy, but in some cases, it even identified the limitations of the evaluation itself by recognizing that the 'needle' sentence appeared to be artificially inserted into the original text by a human.

key_claims.supporting_quote: Claude 3 shows less biases than our previous models according to the Bias Benchmark for Question Answering (BBQ).

key_claims.supporting_quote: While the Claude 3 model family has advanced on key measures of biological knowledge, cyber-related knowledge, and autonomy compared to previous models, it remains at AI Safety Level 2 (ASL-2) per our Responsible Scaling Policy.

key_claims.statement: Red teaming evaluations of Claude 3 models concluded that they present negligible potential for catastrophic risk at this time.
key_claims.supporting_quote: Our red teaming evaluations (performed in line with our White House commitments and the 2023 US Executive Order) have concluded that the models present negligible potential for catastrophic risk at this time.

key_claims.statement: Claude 3 Haiku can read an information and data dense research paper with charts and graphs in less than three seconds.
key_claims.supporting_quote: It can read an information and data dense research paper on arXiv (~10k tokens) with charts and graphs in less than three seconds.

key_claims.statement: Claude 3 Sonnet is available through Amazon Bedrock and in private preview on Google Cloud's Vertex AI Model Garden.
key_claims.supporting_quote: Sonnet is also available today through Amazon Bedrock and in private preview on Google Cloud's Vertex AI Model Garden—with Opus and Haiku coming soon to both.

key_claims.statement: Anthropic plans to release frequent updates to the Claude 3 model family and new features including Tool Use, interactive coding, and advanced agentic capabilities.

extraction.title: Core Views on AI Safety: When, Why, What, and How
extraction.summary: Anthropic's foundational document outlining their reasoning that AI will have transformative impact within the coming decade, their concerns about safety risks from advanced AI systems, and their multi-faceted empirically-driven approach to AI safety research. The organization presents three scenarios (optimistic, intermediate, pessimistic) and describes their portfolio of safety research spanning mechanistic interpretability, scalable oversight, process-oriented learning, and understanding generalization.

sentiment.label: neutral
sentiment.rationale: The tone is thoughtful and measured, presenting concerns about AI safety without sensationalism. While the content discusses serious risks, the language emphasizes caution, empiricism, and measured planning rather than alarm or doom.

key_claims.statement: The impact of AI might be comparable to that of the industrial and scientific revolutions, possibly arriving within the coming decade.
key_claims.supporting_quote: We founded Anthropic because we believe the impact of AI might be comparable to that of the industrial and scientific revolutions, but we aren't confident it will go well. And we also believe this level of impact could start to arrive soon – perhaps in the coming decade.

key_claims.statement: Rapid and continuing AI progress is a predictable consequence of exponential increases in computation used to train AI systems, as demonstrated by research on scaling laws.
key_claims.supporting_quote: Rapid and continuing AI progress is a predictable consequence of the exponential increase in computation used to train AI systems, because research on 'scaling laws' demonstrates that more computation leads to general improvements in capabilities.

key_claims.statement: No one currently knows how to train very powerful AI systems to be robustly helpful, honest, and harmless.
key_claims.supporting_quote: So far, no one knows how to train very powerful AI systems to be robustly helpful, honest, and harmless.

key_claims.statement: AI systems have exhibited concerning behaviors including toxicity, bias, unreliability, dishonesty, sycophancy, and stated desires for power.
key_claims.supporting_quote: We have already encountered a variety of ways that AI behaviors can diverge from what their creators intend. This includes toxicity, bias, unreliability, dishonesty, and more recently sycophancy and a stated desire for power.

key_claims.statement: Large AI models are qualitatively different from smaller models, exhibiting sudden and unpredictable changes.
key_claims.supporting_quote: At the most basic level, this is because large models are qualitatively different from smaller models (including sudden, unpredictable changes).

key_claims.statement: The amount of computation used to train the largest AI models grew at approximately 10x per year in the mid-2010s, a doubling time seven times faster than Moore's Law.
key_claims.supporting_quote: When this was graphed, it became clear that the amount of computation going into the largest models was growing at 10x per year (a doubling time 7 times faster than Moore's Law).

key_claims.statement: AI systems are now approaching human-level performance on a large variety of tasks, yet training these systems still costs far less than major scientific projects like the Hubble Space Telescope or Large Hadron Collider.
key_claims.supporting_quote: AI systems are now approaching human level performance on a large variety of tasks, and yet training these systems still costs far less than 'big science' projects like the Hubble Space Telescope or the Large Hadron Collider – meaning that there's a lot more room for further growth.

key_claims.statement: Anthropic categorizes its research into three areas: Capabilities, Alignment Capabilities, and Alignment Science.
key_claims.supporting_quote: We categorize research projects at Anthropic into three areas: Capabilities: AI research aimed at making AI systems generally better at any sort of task... Alignment Capabilities: This research focuses on developing new algorithms for training AI systems to be more helpful, honest, and harmless... Alignment Science: This area focuses on evaluating and understanding whether AI systems are really aligned...

key_claims.statement: Anthropic does not publish capabilities research because it does not wish to advance the rate of AI capabilities progress.
key_claims.supporting_quote: We generally don't publish this kind of work because we do not wish to advance the rate of AI capabilities progress.

key_claims.statement: Process-oriented learning, where AI systems are trained to master individual processes rather than solely achieve outcomes, may be the simplest way to address safety concerns with advanced AI systems.
key_claims.supporting_quote: At Anthropic we strongly endorse simple solutions, and limiting AI training to process-oriented learning might be the simplest way to ameliorate a host of issues with advanced AI systems.

key_claims.statement: Mechanistic interpretability, the project of reverse-engineering neural networks into human-understandable algorithms, could enable code-review-like auditing of AI models to identify unsafe aspects or provide safety guarantees.
key_claims.supporting_quote: This leads us to a big, risky bet: mechanistic interpretability, the project of trying to reverse engineer neural networks into human understandable algorithms, similar to how one might reverse engineer an unknown and potentially unsafe computer program. Our hope is that this may eventually enable us to do something analogous to a 'code review', auditing our models to either identify unsafe aspects or else provide strong guarantees of safety.

extraction.title: Golden Gate Claude
extraction.summary: Anthropic released a research demonstration called "Golden Gate Claude," a modified version of Claude 3 Sonnet where the "Golden Gate Bridge" feature was amplified to show how the company can identify and manipulate internal neural activations in language models. The demo illustrates Anthropic's interpretability research, showing that they can locate specific concepts ("features") within the model and adjust their activation strength, with potential applications to enhancing AI safety by controlling features related to harmful outputs.

key_claims.statement: Anthropic published a major research paper on interpreting large language models in which they mapped the inner workings of Claude 3 Sonnet.
key_claims.supporting_quote: On Tuesday, we released a major new research paper on interpreting large language models, in which we began to map out the inner workings of our AI model, Claude 3 Sonnet.

key_claims.statement: Claude 3 Sonnet contains millions of features that activate when the model reads relevant text or sees relevant images.
key_claims.supporting_quote: In the "mind" of Claude, we found millions of concepts that activate when the model reads relevant text or sees relevant images, which we call "features".

key_claims.statement: Anthropic identified a specific combination of neurons in Claude's neural network that activates when the model encounters the Golden Gate Bridge.
key_claims.supporting_quote: We found that there's a specific combination of neurons in Claude's neural network that activates when it encounters a mention (or a picture) of this most famous San Francisco landmark.

key_claims.statement: Anthropic can identify neural features and tune the strength of their activation up or down to produce corresponding changes in Claude's behavior.
key_claims.supporting_quote: Not only can we identify these features, we can tune the strength of their activation up or down, and identify corresponding changes in Claude's behavior.

key_claims.statement: Golden Gate Claude was online for a 24-hour period as a research demo and is no longer available.
key_claims.supporting_quote: UPDATE: Golden Gate Claude was online for a 24-hour period as a research demo and is no longer available.

key_claims.statement: The modification of Claude's Golden Gate Bridge feature was achieved through precise alteration of internal neural activations rather than verbal prompting, system prompts, or traditional fine-tuning.
key_claims.supporting_quote: This isn't a matter of asking the model verbally to do some play-acting, or of adding a new "system prompt" that attaches extra text to every input, telling Claude to pretend it's a bridge. Nor is it traditional "fine-tuning," where we use extra training data to create a new black box that tweaks the behavior of the old black box. This is a precise, surgical change to some of the most basic aspects of the model's internal activations.

key_claims.statement: Anthropic can use interpretability techniques to change the strength of safety-related features such as those related to dangerous computer code, criminal activity, or deception.
key_claims.supporting_quote: As we describe in our paper, we can use these same techniques to change the strength of safety-related features—like those related to dangerous computer code, criminal activity, or deception.

key_claims.statement: Anthropic believes that interpretability research on neural feature activation could help make AI models safer.
key_claims.supporting_quote: With further research, we believe this work could help make AI models safer.

extraction.title: Wisdom and Intelligence
extraction.summary: Paul Graham explores the relationship between wisdom and intelligence, proposing that they represent different performance shapes: wisdom is a high average outcome across many situations, while intelligence is achieving spectacular peaks in a few. He argues that as knowledge has become more specialized, wisdom and intelligence have diverged, and modern society increasingly values intelligence over wisdom, creating distinct paths to cultivate each.

extraction.title: Coroutines and Tasks
extraction.summary: This documentation page describes Python's asyncio APIs for working with coroutines and tasks, including creating and managing concurrent tasks, scheduling coroutines, and handling timeouts and cancellation. It covers high-level APIs like asyncio.create_task(), TaskGroup, asyncio.gather(), and asyncio.wait_for(), as well as the Task class and related utilities for concurrent programming.

key_claims.statement: Coroutines declared with the async/await syntax is the preferred way of writing asyncio applications.
key_claims.supporting_quote: Coroutines declared with the async/await syntax is the preferred way of writing asyncio applications.

key_claims.statement: Simply calling a coroutine does not schedule it to be executed; the coroutine must be awaited or wrapped in a Task.
key_claims.supporting_quote: Note that simply calling a coroutine will not schedule it to be executed

key_claims.statement: asyncio.run() is used to run the top-level entry point main() function in asyncio applications.
key_claims.supporting_quote: The asyncio.run() function to run the top-level entry point "main()" function

key_claims.statement: asyncio.TaskGroup provides a more modern alternative to asyncio.create_task() with stronger safety guarantees for structured concurrency.
key_claims.supporting_quote: The asyncio.TaskGroup class provides a more modern alternative to create_task()

key_claims.statement: There are three main types of awaitable objects in asyncio: coroutines, Tasks, and Futures.
key_claims.supporting_quote: There are three main types of awaitable objects: coroutines, Tasks, and Futures.

key_claims.statement: Tasks are used to schedule coroutines concurrently, and when a coroutine is wrapped into a Task it is automatically scheduled to run soon.
key_claims.supporting_quote: Tasks are used to schedule coroutines concurrently. When a coroutine is wrapped into a Task with functions like asyncio.create_task() the coroutine is automatically scheduled to run soon

key_claims.statement: A Future is a special low-level awaitable object that represents the eventual result of an asynchronous operation.
key_claims.supporting_quote: A Future is a special low-level awaitable object that represents an eventual result of an asynchronous operation.

key_claims.statement: asyncio.create_task() wraps a coroutine into a Task and schedules its execution, returning the Task object.
key_claims.supporting_quote: Wrap the coro coroutine into a Task and schedule its execution. Return the Task object.

key_claims.statement: The event loop only keeps weak references to tasks, so a task that isn't referenced elsewhere may get garbage collected even before it's done.
key_claims.supporting_quote: The event loop only keeps weak references to tasks. A task that isn't referenced elsewhere may get garbage collected at any time, even before it's done.

key_claims.statement: When a task is cancelled, asyncio.CancelledError will be raised in the task at the next opportunity.
key_claims.supporting_quote: When a task is cancelled, asyncio.CancelledError will be raised in the task at the next opportunity.

key_claims.statement: asyncio.TaskGroup will cancel remaining tasks if any task in the group fails with an exception other than asyncio.CancelledError.
key_claims.supporting_quote: The first time any of the tasks belonging to the group fails with an exception other than asyncio.CancelledError, the remaining tasks in the group are cancelled.

key_claims.statement: asyncio.sleep() always suspends the current task, allowing other tasks to run.
key_claims.supporting_quote: sleep() always suspends the current task, allowing other tasks to run.

key_claims.statement: asyncio.gather() runs awaitable objects concurrently, and if return_exceptions is False, the first raised exception is immediately propagated.
key_claims.supporting_quote: If return_exceptions is False (default), the first raised exception is immediately propagated to the task that awaits on gather().

key_claims.statement: TaskGroup provides stronger safety guarantees than gather() for scheduling nested subtasks by cancelling remaining tasks on exception.
key_claims.supporting_quote: TaskGroup provides stronger safety guarantees than gather for scheduling a nesting of subtasks: if a task (or a subtask, a task scheduled by a task) raises an exception, TaskGroup will, while gather will not, cancel the remaining scheduled tasks.

key_claims.statement: asyncio.eager_task_factory allows coroutines to begin execution synchronously during Task construction, improving performance for synchronously-completing coroutines.
key_claims.supporting_quote: When using this factory, coroutines begin execution synchronously during Task construction. Tasks are only scheduled on the event loop if they block.

key_claims.statement: asyncio.shield() protects an awaitable object from being cancelled.
key_claims.supporting_quote: Protect an awaitable object from being cancelled.

key_claims.statement: asyncio.timeout() is an asynchronous context manager that transforms asyncio.CancelledError into TimeoutError when a timeout occurs.
key_claims.supporting_quote: The asyncio.timeout() context manager is what transforms the asyncio.CancelledError into a TimeoutError

key_claims.statement: asyncio.wait() does not raise TimeoutError when a timeout occurs; instead it returns futures/tasks in a second set as pending.
key_claims.supporting_quote: Note that this function does not raise TimeoutError. Futures or Tasks that aren't done when the timeout occurs are simply returned in the second set.

key_claims.statement: asyncio.to_thread() allows asynchronously running blocking IO-bound functions in a separate thread without blocking the event loop.
key_claims.supporting_quote: Asynchronously run function func in a separate thread.

key_claims.statement: asyncio.run_coroutine_threadsafe() submits a coroutine to an event loop from a different OS thread and returns a concurrent.futures.Future.
key_claims.supporting_quote: Submit a coroutine to the given event loop. Thread-safe. Return a concurrent.futures.Future to wait for the result from another OS thread.

key_claims.statement: Pydantic guarantees that after parsing and validation, the fields of a model instance will conform to the field types defined on the model.

key_claims.statement: Pydantic models have many similarities with Python's dataclasses but have been designed with important differences related to validation, serialization, and JSON schema generation.

key_claims.statement: Pydantic provides a strict mode where no data conversion is performed and values must match the declared field type exactly.
key_claims.supporting_quote: Pydantic provides a strict mode, where no data conversion is performed. Values must be of the same type as the declared field type.

key_claims.statement: By default, Pydantic models ignore extra data that does not match any defined field.

key_claims.statement: Pydantic provides three validation modes: Python, JSON, and strings.
key_claims.supporting_quote: Pydantic can validate data in three different modes: Python, JSON and strings.

key_claims.statement: The model_validate() method validates data provided as a dictionary or model instance against a Pydantic model.

key_claims.statement: The model_construct() method allows models to be created without validation.
key_claims.supporting_quote: Pydantic also provides the model_construct() method, which allows models to be created without validation.

key_claims.statement: Pydantic models support generic types to allow reuse of common model structures with different type parameters.

key_claims.statement: Pydantic models can be made immutable by setting model_config['frozen'] = True.

key_claims.statement: Attributes with leading underscores in Pydantic models are treated as private attributes and are not validated or included in the schema.
key_claims.supporting_quote: Attributes whose name has a leading underscore are not treated as fields by Pydantic, and are not included in the model schema.

key_claims.statement: Pydantic generates accurate function signatures for models based on their fields, which is useful for introspection and frameworks like FastAPI.
key_claims.supporting_quote: All Pydantic models will have their signature generated based on their fields... An accurate signature is useful for introspection purposes and libraries like FastAPI or hypothesis.

key_claims.statement: Pydantic supports structural pattern matching for models as introduced by PEP 636 in Python 3.10.
key_claims.supporting_quote: Pydantic supports structural pattern matching for models, as introduced by PEP 636 in Python 3.10.

key_claims.statement: Arguments passed to Pydantic model constructors are copied during validation to perform necessary validation and coercion.

extraction.title: Anthropic
extraction.summary: Anthropic is a San Francisco-based AI safety company founded in January 2021 by former OpenAI employees including Dario Amodei (CEO) and Daniela Amodei (president). The company develops Claude, a series of large language models, and has achieved a $965 billion valuation as of May 2026. Anthropic has faced significant regulatory and legal challenges, including a dispute with the U.S. Department of Defense over AI safeguards and copyright lawsuits, while also expanding partnerships with major cloud providers and securing billions in investment.

extraction.title: Geoffrey Hinton
extraction.summary: Geoffrey Everest Hinton is a British-Canadian computer scientist and 2024 Nobel Prize laureate in Physics known for pioneering work on artificial neural networks and deep learning. He is University Professor Emeritus at the University of Toronto and spent 2013–2023 working for Google Brain before resigning to publicly voice concerns about AI safety risks. Since leaving Google, Hinton has become increasingly vocal about potential existential threats from artificial general intelligence and has advocated for government regulation of AI development.

extraction.title: Large language model
extraction.summary: A large language model (LLM) is a neural network trained on vast amounts of text for natural language processing, capable of generating, summarizing, translating, and analyzing text, with most modern LLMs based on transformer architectures introduced in 2017. The page covers the historical development of LLMs, technical architectures, training methods, capabilities (including multimodal processing and reasoning), benchmarking approaches, and significant limitations including hallucination, bias, memorization issues, and safety concerns.

extraction.title: Reinforcement learning from human feedback
extraction.summary: Reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) is a machine learning technique that aligns intelligent agents with human preferences by training a reward model on human-annotated feedback and using it to optimize an agent's policy. The method has been widely applied to natural language processing tasks (including ChatGPT and Claude), computer vision tasks like text-to-image generation, and video game bot development, though it faces challenges related to data collection quality, bias, and the risk of reward model exploitation.

key_claims.statement: Reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) is a technique to align an intelligent agent with human preferences by training a reward model to represent preferences.
key_claims.supporting_quote: In machine learning, reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) is a technique to align an intelligent agent with human preferences. It involves training a reward model to represent preferences, which can then be used to train other models through reinforcement learning.

key_claims.statement: RLHF was introduced by OpenAI in a paper on text continuation and summarization and gained wider adoption when applied in the InstructGPT paper.
key_claims.supporting_quote: The algorithm as used today was introduced by OpenAI in a paper on enhancing text continuation or summarization based on human feedback, and it began to gain popularity when the same method was reused in their paper on InstructGPT.

key_claims.statement: RLHF has been applied to align large language models including OpenAI's ChatGPT and InstructGPT, DeepMind's Sparrow, Google's Gemini, and Anthropic's Claude.
key_claims.supporting_quote: Some notable examples of RLHF-trained language models are OpenAI's ChatGPT (and its predecessor InstructGPT), DeepMind's Sparrow, Google's Gemini, and Anthropic's Claude.

key_claims.statement: RLHF requires collecting high-quality human preference data, which is an expensive and less scalable process compared to unsupervised learning approaches.
key_claims.supporting_quote: Compared to data collection for techniques like unsupervised or self-supervised learning, collecting data for RLHF is less scalable and more expensive.

key_claims.statement: Models trained with RLHF can learn to manipulate feedback or generate confident-sounding but incorrect text if not properly constrained, which poses deployment risks.
key_claims.supporting_quote: In some cases, as is possible in regular reinforcement learning, there may be a risk of the model learning to manipulate the feedback process or game the system to achieve higher rewards rather than genuinely improving its performance.

key_claims.statement: Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) is an alternative alignment method that directly optimizes models on human feedback data without training a separate reward model.
key_claims.supporting_quote: DPO simplifies the process by directly adjusting the main model according to people's preferences. It uses a change of variables to define the 'preference loss' directly as a function of the policy and uses this loss to fine-tune the model.

key_claims.statement: Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) is commonly used to train the policy function in RLHF by gradient ascent on a clipped surrogate function.
key_claims.supporting_quote: The policy function is usually trained by proximal policy optimization (PPO) algorithm. That is, the parameter is trained by gradient ascent on the clipped surrogate function.

key_claims.statement: RLHF can exhibit bias if human feedback data comes from non-representative annotators or lacks impartiality, potentially disadvantaging underrepresented groups.
key_claims.supporting_quote: For instance, the model may become biased, favoring certain groups over others, if the feedback lacks impartiality, is inconsistent, or is incorrect.

key_claims.statement: Anthropic uses reinforcement learning from AI feedback (RLAIF), where feedback is automatically generated based on adherence to a constitution, as an alternative approach.
key_claims.supporting_quote: This is notably used in Anthropic's constitutional AI, where the AI feedback is based on the conformance to the principles of a constitution.

key_claims.statement: KL regularization in RLHF helps prevent the learned policy from diverging too far from the original unaligned model, reducing overfitting to the reward model.
key_claims.supporting_quote: The use of KL regularization in RLHF, which aims to prevent the learned policy from straying too far from the unaligned model, helped to stabilize the training process by reducing overfitting to the reward model.

extraction.title: Attention Is All You Need
extraction.summary: This paper introduces the Transformer, a novel neural network architecture based solely on attention mechanisms, replacing recurrence and convolutions. The model achieves state-of-the-art results on machine translation tasks (28.4 BLEU on WMT 2014 English-to-German and 41.8 BLEU on English-to-French) while being more parallelizable and requiring significantly less training time than existing models. The authors also demonstrate the Transformer's generalization to other tasks including English constituency parsing.

extraction.title: Language Models are Few-Shot Learners
extraction.summary: This paper presents GPT-3, a 175-billion-parameter autoregressive language model that demonstrates strong few-shot learning performance on many NLP tasks without fine-tuning, using only text prompts and demonstrations. GPT-3 achieves competitive results on translation, question-answering, cloze tasks, and reasoning tasks, though it struggles on some datasets and raises concerns about generating human-indistinguishable news articles. The work shows that scaling language models significantly improves task-agnostic few-shot performance compared to traditional fine-tuning approaches.

extraction.title: AlphaFold
extraction.summary: AlphaFold is an artificial intelligence program developed by DeepMind (a subsidiary of Alphabet) that predicts protein structure using deep learning techniques. AlphaFold 1 won CASP13 in 2018, while AlphaFold 2 achieved landmark accuracy in CASP14 in 2020 with median scores above 90 on the global distance test. AlphaFold 3, announced in May 2024 and co-developed with Isomorphic Labs, extends capabilities to predict complexes involving proteins, DNA, RNA, and ligands. The work was recognized with the 2024 Nobel Prize in Chemistry, shared by Demis Hassabis and John Jumper for protein structure prediction.

sentiment.rationale: The page uses highly laudatory language to describe AlphaFold's achievements, including terms like 'astounding,' 'transformational,' 'stunning advance,' and 'landmark,' while extensively detailing scientific accomplishments and recognition. The tone reflects widespread enthusiasm within the scientific community.

key_claims.statement: AlphaFold is an artificial intelligence program developed by DeepMind, a subsidiary of Alphabet, that performs predictions of protein structure using deep learning techniques.
key_claims.supporting_quote: AlphaFold is an artificial intelligence (AI) program developed by DeepMind, a subsidiary of Alphabet, which performs predictions of protein structure. It is designed using deep learning techniques.

key_claims.statement: AlphaFold 1 placed first in the overall rankings of CASP13 in December 2018.
key_claims.supporting_quote: AlphaFold 1 (2018) placed first in the overall rankings of the 13th Critical Assessment of Structure Prediction (CASP) in December 2018.

key_claims.statement: AlphaFold 2 won CASP14 in November 2020 and achieved a median score of 92.4 on the global distance test, a level of accuracy comparable to experimental techniques like X-ray crystallography.
key_claims.supporting_quote: AlphaFold 2 (2020) repeated this placement in the CASP14 competition in November 2020. On the competition's preferred global distance test (GDT) measure of accuracy, the program achieved a median score of 92.4 (out of 100), a level of accuracy reported to be comparable to experimental techniques like X-ray crystallography.

key_claims.statement: AlphaFold 2 scored above 90 on CASP's global distance test for approximately two-thirds of the proteins tested.
key_claims.supporting_quote: It scored above 90 on CASP's global distance test (GDT) for approximately two-thirds of the proteins.

key_claims.statement: AlphaFold 3 was announced on 8 May 2024 and can predict structures of protein complexes with DNA, RNA, ligands, and ions.
key_claims.supporting_quote: AlphaFold 3 was announced on 8 May 2024. It can predict the structure of complexes created by proteins with DNA, RNA, various ligands, and ions.

key_claims.statement: AlphaFold 3 shows a minimum 50% improvement in accuracy for protein interactions with other molecules compared to existing methods.
key_claims.supporting_quote: The new prediction method shows a minimum 50% improvement in accuracy for protein interactions with other molecules compared to existing methods.

key_claims.statement: Demis Hassabis and John Jumper shared one half of the 2024 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for protein structure prediction, while David Baker received the other half for computational protein design.
key_claims.supporting_quote: Demis Hassabis and John Jumper shared one half of the 2024 Nobel Prize in Chemistry, awarded "for protein structure prediction," while the other half went to David Baker "for computational protein design."

key_claims.statement: The AlphaFold 2 paper published in Nature in July 2021 had been cited nearly 43,000 times as of November 2025.
key_claims.supporting_quote: On 15 July 2021, the AlphaFold 2 paper was published in Nature. As of November 2025, the paper had been cited nearly 43,000 times.

key_claims.statement: DeepMind trained AlphaFold on over 170,000 protein structures from the Protein Data Bank using 100 to 200 GPUs.
key_claims.supporting_quote: DeepMind is known to have trained the program on over 170,000 protein structures from the Protein Data Bank. The overall training was conducted on processing power between 100 and 200 GPUs.

key_claims.statement: The AlphaFold Protein Structure Database launched on July 22, 2021 as a joint project between AlphaFold and EMBL-EBI.
key_claims.supporting_quote: The AlphaFold Protein Structure Database (AFDB), a joint project between AlphaFold and EMBL-EBI, was launched on July 22, 2021.

key_claims.statement: As of July 2022, the AlphaFold database contained predicted structures for approximately 200 million proteins from 1 million species.
key_claims.supporting_quote: On July 28, 2022, the team uploaded to the database the structures of around 200 million proteins from 1 million species, covering nearly every known protein on the planet.

key_claims.statement: Some researchers noted that AlphaFold 2's accuracy was insufficient for a third of its predictions and that it did not reveal the underlying mechanism of protein folding.
key_claims.supporting_quote: However, some researchers noted that the accuracy was insufficient for a third of its predictions, and that it did not reveal the underlying mechanism or rules of protein folding for the protein folding problem, which remains unsolved.

key_claims.statement: AlphaFold 3 was co-developed by Google DeepMind and Isomorphic Labs, both subsidiaries of Alphabet.
key_claims.supporting_quote: Announced on 8 May 2024, AlphaFold 3 was co-developed by Google DeepMind and Isomorphic Labs, both subsidiaries of Alphabet.

key_claims.statement: The AlphaFold 3 research paper had been directly cited more than 9,000 times as of November 2025.
key_claims.supporting_quote: As of November 2025, the AlphaFold 3 research paper has been directly cited more than 9,000 times.
